mnemonics.title: Remember Schema Order
mnemonics.content: ICE - Internal → Conceptual → External (from bottom to top, think of ICE as building from foundation)
mnemonics.usage: Recall the three schema levels in order from lowest to highest

mnemonics.title: Data Independence Types
mnemonics.content: PEL - Physical External Logical (Physical first, then External mapping, then Logical)
mnemonics.usage: Remember the two types: Physical and Logical Data Independence

mnemonics.title: External Schema Purpose
mnemonics.usage: Remember that external schema represents user-specific views

acronyms.expansion: American National Standards Institute - Standards Planning and Requirements Committee
acronyms.usage: The committee that proposed the Three-Schema Architecture in 1975
Software system that manages databases using three-schema architecture
Used to define schemas at conceptual and internal levels
Used to manipulate data through external schemas

visualTips.visualization: Draw a three-layer pyramid: Bottom (Internal - Physical storage), Middle (Conceptual - Logical structure), Top (External - User views). Arrows connect them showing data flow and mapping.
visualTips.purpose: Visualize the hierarchical relationship between schema levels

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a sandwich: Physical layer (bottom bread), Conceptual layer (filling), External layer (top bread). Changing bottom bread doesn't affect top bread (Physical Independence). Changing filling affects top bread unless properly mapped (Logical Independence).
visualTips.purpose: Understand how changes at one level affect others

visualTips.visualization: Draw two-way arrows: External ↔ Conceptual (External/Conceptual Mapping) and Conceptual ↔ Internal (Conceptual/Internal Mapping). These arrows represent transformations.
visualTips.purpose: Remember that mappings exist in both directions

analogies.analogy: Like a building: Internal Schema = Blueprint (how it's actually built with bricks, beams), Conceptual Schema = Architect's design (floor plan, room connections), External Schema = Different person's view (resident sees living room, office worker sees workspace).
Like a TV remote: Changing batteries (physical change) doesn't change the channel you're watching (logical/content). Similarly, changing storage doesn't change what data means.
Like different passengers in a car seeing different things: driver sees road, passengers see scenery, back seat sees inside - all from same car but different views.
